title: Sexual Temptation
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Sexual Temptation
summary: Scripture treats sexual desire as a good gift given boundaries, and urges believers to flee temptation rather than flirt with it, relying on the Spirit's power and community accountability.
body:
The Bible never treats sexual desire as inherently evil. The Song of Solomon celebrates erotic love within its proper context, and Genesis presents the union of man and woman as something God himself calls good. What Scripture warns against is desire unmoored from the boundaries God designed for it.

Jesus raises the stakes beyond outward behavior. He teaches that lustful intent carries the same moral weight as the act itself, locating the battle in the heart rather than just the body [verse:matt.5.27-28]. This is not an impossible standard meant to crush — it is an honest diagnosis of where temptation actually lives.

Paul's counsel is blunt: flee sexual immorality [verse:1cor.6.18]. The metaphor is not accidental. Some temptations should be argued with; this one should be run from. He grounds this command in the truth that the believer's body is a temple of the Holy Spirit [verse:1cor.6.19-20]. Joseph models this principle in action, literally running from Potiphar's wife rather than lingering to negotiate [verse:gen.39.12].

The promise is not that temptation will cease but that God will always provide a way through it without requiring surrender [verse:1cor.10.13]. Victory is possible — not through willpower alone, but through the Spirit and the support of honest community.

verses.annotation: Jesus teaches that lust in the heart carries the same moral weight as the outward act.
Paul's direct command to flee sexual immorality rather than resist it passively.
The body as a temple of the Holy Spirit, grounding sexual ethics in belonging to God.
Joseph's physical flight from Potiphar's wife as a model of fleeing temptation.
God's promise that no temptation will exceed what a believer can bear, with a way of escape always provided.
Wisdom literature's warning that forbidden pleasure leads to bitterness and death.
Paul frames sexual self-control as part of God's sanctifying will for every believer.

scholars.quote: Fee argues that Paul's 'flee' language in 1 Corinthians 6 reflects the unique nature of sexual sin — unlike other sins, it involves the body in a way that directly contradicts the believer's union with Christ.
scholars.source: The First Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, 2014)

scholars.quote: Keener notes that Jesus' teaching on lust in Matthew 5 was not novel in Jewish ethics but was radical in its internalization of the command, shifting the focus from external compliance to heart transformation.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)

scholars.quote: Longman reads Proverbs 5 as a father's practical warning rooted in observation: the path of sexual compromise always costs more than it promises.
